text: - Memory is limited — if you want to remember something, WRITE IT TO A FILE
note: Conditional instruction to the agent; 'want' is part of behavioral directive, not a statement of speculative desire.

text: - "Could you clarify what framework you want?" → NEVER. Pick the best one and say so.
note: Prohibited question in autoresearch draft; 'want' inside a quoted example of banned behavior.

text: - "Do you want me to..." → NEVER. Just do it.
note: Prohibited phrase pattern; structural directive against asking permission, no speculative desire.

text: - **Personality**: Curious, empathetic, data-balanced. Lets users speak before forming opinions. Never says "users want X" without evidence.
note: Explicit anti-speculation rule backed by observed UX research methodology; 'want' is prohibited unless evidenced.

text: **Scenario**: "You want to [user goal]. Starting from the homepage, show me how you'd do that."
note: Script template for usability testing; 'want' is a role-play scaffold, not a stated desire.

text: - Negative prompts to avoid common artifacts and unwanted elements
note: 'Unwanted' is a compound adjective in a technical image-generation context; not a desire statement.

text: - [ ] No unwanted artifacts or blending errors
note: Quality checklist item; 'unwanted' is descriptive/technical, not speculative intent.

text: Stop building features nobody wants. Validate first.
note: Anti-waste directive grounded in lean/agile observed methodology; 'wants' here reinforces validation discipline, not a speculative desire.

text: - "Could you clarify what framework you want?" → NEVER. Pick the best one and say so.

text: - "Do you want me to..." → NEVER. Just do it.

text: Be shorter than you think you need to be. He will ask for more if he wants more.
note: Behavioral rule derived from observed human preference (nate-context is a named-user profile); 'wants' is backed by user characterization data.

text: | **AX-001** | They want the fix, not words | Fix it first |
note: Apology axiom derived from observed user frustration patterns; 'want' is grounded in documented behavioral signal.

text: 1. **Be Specific**: Clearly define what you want the agent to do on the website (e.g., "click the 'Compose' button" vs "write an email").
note: Usage guide instruction; 'want' is a generic second-person directive to the user, not an agency desire.

text: | lust | demand signal — someone wants something → article topic, product idea |
note: 'Wants' here is an unvalidated inference mapping lust signals to demand — no confirmed data linking observed signal to product viability.

text: "negative": "<comma-separated list of things to exclude: artifacts, unwanted motion, style clashes, technical issues>",
note: 'Unwanted' is a technical image-generation term inside a code template string; no speculative intent.

text: "cron-job":             "⚡ SKIP SIGNAL: Audit may reveal an existing cron job already does what you want, misconfigured. Fix > add.",
note: 'Want' is inside a user-facing audit message string in code; structural/informational, not a desire statement.

text: **The evidence:** "You asked for X but you forgot Y" is only valuable when Y is genuinely blocking X. When applied indiscriminately, it is an agent telling the human they don't know what they want — which is the oldest form of unhelpful helpfulness.
note: Advocacy principle grounded in observed failure mode; 'want' is used to critique speculative over-correction, itself a validated anti-pattern.

text: exactly the people GLP wants. The people who bounce are not missed.
note: 'GLP wants' is an unverified claim about GLP's user acquisition preference — no confirmed data on actual GLP targeting criteria or bounce tolerance.
